metadata.title: Assemble & Audit for Coherence
metadata.playlist: Make It Count — The Complete Brand System
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Capstone — Complete Brand System (200 pts, due 2026-08-14)

beats.narration_text: Ciao — this is Liam. Episode 3 of 6. Coherence is 40 of 200 points — and nothing in the capstone rub says forgiveness for 'almost matching.' This episode is the assembly episode: you gather all six pieces, you run the coherence audit, and you use the Assignment 7 style guide as the yardstick. Nothing out of place. Same family across every touchpoint. Let's go.

props.command: Audit all six capstone pieces against my A7 style guide. Flag any inconsistency across touchpoints.
props.runningText: coherence is 40 points — nothing out of place, same family across every touchpoint…
output: Path-specific bundle and social content listed
Coherence audit against A7 style guide — inconsistencies flagged

beats.act: identity system — logo, palette, type, style guide
beats.narration_text: The identity system. This came from Assignment 7, and it is the yardstick everything else gets measured against. Four components, all required. First: the logo and its variations — primary lockup, mark only, wordmark, light version, dark version. At least two variations are expected in a professional system. Second: the palette with exact hex values — not 'blue and cream,' but the actual hex codes that appear in every other piece. Third: the primary and secondary typefaces by name — not 'a sans-serif,' the actual family names and weights you used. Fourth: a style guide summary, one to two slides that a designer you have never met could pick up and use to stay on brand. If your style guide still says 'we might use this color' rather than 'this is the color,' it is not finished. The identity system is not what you presented in Assignment 7 — it is the current, post-iteration version that reflects every refinement you made in Assignments 8 and 9. If the website changed the palette, update the guide.

props.artifactHeading: The yardstick every other piece is measured against
artifactLines: Logo + variations: primary lockup · mark only · wordmark · light/dark versions (≥2 required).
Palette: exact hex values — not 'blue and cream,' the actual codes used everywhere.
Type: primary + secondary family names and weights — by name, not category.
Style guide: 1–2 slides a stranger can use to stay on brand.
Use the current post-A8/A9 version — if the website changed the palette, update the guide.
props.sparkLine: The style guide is the yardstick. If it's still a draft, the audit will fail.

beats.act: strategy doc — UVP, pillars, personas, competitive positioning
beats.narration_text: The strategy document. This came from Assignments 6 and 5. Four components that must be present and current. First: the unique value proposition — one to two sentences that name what you do, who you do it for, and what makes your approach different. If your UVP still sounds like a generic mission statement from a template, it is not finished. Second: three to five brand pillars — the values or principles that every content and design decision should be traceable back to. Third: at least two personas — specific enough that you can point to a real piece of content and say 'this was written for Persona One because of this specific need.' Fourth: competitive positioning — where you sit relative to two or three alternatives, stated specifically, not with a vague claim that you are 'better.' The strategy document is not a slide deck — it is a reference document that the rest of your capstone should be consistent with. If you read your strategy document and then look at your website copy, do they describe the same brand?

props.artifactHeading: The reference the rest of the system should agree with
artifactLines: UVP: 1–2 sentences — what you do · who for · what's different. Not a template mission statement.
Pillars: 3–5 values every content and design decision traces back to.
Personas: ≥2 — specific enough to point at a piece of content and say 'this was for Persona 1.'
Competitive positioning: where you sit vs 2–3 alternatives — specific claim, not 'we're better.'
Test: read the strategy doc, then read the website copy. Do they describe the same brand?
props.sparkLine: If the website copy and the strategy doc disagree, fix one of them.

beats.act: path-specific bundle — Path A and Path B requirements
beats.narration_text: The path-specific bundle. This depends on which path you chose in Assignments 6 and have been building since. Path A is the personal brand path. The required bundle: two resumes — a traditional format and a modern or visual format, both optimized for your target industry. An optimized LinkedIn profile — headline, about section, and featured section current with your brand positioning. Three to five portfolio samples — work that demonstrates the skills your brand positions you around. Path B is the startup brand path. The required bundle: a three-slide pitch deck — problem, solution, and why now or why you. An optimized LinkedIn page for the startup — not your personal profile, the company page. One to two pages of tool documentation — written for a user who has never seen the tool, describing the key features and the methodology. Both paths also require: three to five social posts consistent with the brand voice and visual identity, and two brand stories from Assignment 9 — both complete, both using distinct frameworks, both traceable to the brand values.

props.artifactHeading: Both paths also require: 3–5 social posts + 2 brand stories
artifactLines: Path A: 2 resumes (traditional + modern) · optimized LinkedIn · 3–5 portfolio samples.
Path B: 3-slide pitch deck (problem/solution/why now) · LinkedIn company page · 1–2 pp tool docs.
Both paths: 3–5 social posts (brand voice + visual identity consistent).
Both paths: 2 brand stories from A9 — distinct frameworks · both traceable to brand values.
Social posts and stories must match the style guide palette, type, and voice.
props.sparkLine: Path A = personal proof. Path B = startup proof. Both need social + stories.

beats.act: coherence audit — the style guide as yardstick
beats.narration_text: The coherence audit. Forty points. The audit is not 'does it look good' — the audit is 'does every touchpoint use the same family of palette, type, and voice as the Assignment 7 style guide?' The brandy prompt pattern: paste your style guide values and a description of each touchpoint into Claude, and ask it to flag any inconsistency. Website header type does not match the primary typeface in the guide? Flag. Social post uses a color that does not appear in the hex palette? Flag. LinkedIn about section sounds like a different person than the brand stories? Flag. The most common coherence failure is the website drifting during build — you tweak a color to make something look better on screen, and two months later that color is different from the one in the guide. The second most common failure is the social posts being written in a different voice than the website copy — too casual, too formal, or using different vocabulary for the same concept. The fix for both is simple: the style guide is the authority. If a touchpoint disagrees with the guide, the touchpoint changes.

props.artifactHeading: A7 style guide is the authority. Every touchpoint answers to it.
artifactLines: Audit question: does every touchpoint use the same palette · type · voice as the A7 guide?
Brandy prompt: paste style guide values + each touchpoint description → Claude flags inconsistencies.
Common failure 1: website color drifted during build — check hex against guide.
Common failure 2: social posts in a different voice than website copy.
Fix rule: the style guide is the authority — if a touchpoint disagrees, the touchpoint changes.
props.sparkLine: Not 'does it look good' — does it match the guide? 40 points on that answer.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Assemble and Audit for Coherence. The identity system is the yardstick — logo variations, palette with exact hex values, type family names, and a current style guide. The strategy document anchors everything: UVP, pillars, personas, and competitive positioning, all current with what you actually built. The path-specific bundle depends on your track — Path A is resumes, LinkedIn, and portfolio; Path B is pitch deck, LinkedIn page, and tool docs. Both paths need social posts and brand stories consistent with the brand. The coherence audit is forty points: use the brandy prompt to flag every touchpoint against the style guide. The style guide is the authority — if a touchpoint disagrees, the touchpoint changes. Episode 4 is the presentation deck.

props.artifactHeading: Assembled system · coherence audit · key rules
artifactLines: Identity system: logo variations · hex palette · type names · current style guide.
Strategy doc: UVP · pillars · ≥2 personas · competitive positioning — current version.
Path bundle: A (resumes + LinkedIn + portfolio) or B (pitch deck + LinkedIn + tool docs).
Both paths: 3–5 social posts + 2 brand stories — consistent with brand voice and palette.
Coherence audit (40 pts): brandy prompt against A7 guide. Style guide = authority.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Run the coherence audit before you build the deck. Paste this prompt into Claude with your own style guide values and touchpoint descriptions. The prompt reads: I am auditing my INFO 7375 capstone for brand coherence. I will give you my Assignment 7 style guide values and a description of each touchpoint. For each touchpoint, flag any inconsistency with the style guide across three categories: palette — does it use colors outside the hex values I specified; typography — does it use typefaces not in my primary or secondary family; and voice — does the written content feel like a different person or brand than the others. Here are my style guide values. Primary hex: [paste]. Secondary hex values: [paste, up to four]. Primary typeface: [name and weight]. Secondary typeface: [name and weight]. Brand voice in three adjectives: [list]. Now here are my touchpoints. Website header and navigation: [describe type, colors used, voice sample]. Website body copy: [describe]. LinkedIn headline and about section: [paste or describe]. Social posts: [paste one or two, describe the rest]. Brand stories from A9: [describe voice and tone]. Deck slides: [describe if built, or 'not yet']. For each touchpoint, tell me: consistent, minor drift, or major inconsistency — and for any drift or inconsistency, name the exact fix. After you get the audit, make every fix Claude flags as major before you move to Episode 4.

props.command: A7 style guide: primary hex · secondary hex · primary type · secondary type · brand voice (3 adjectives). Touchpoints: website · LinkedIn · social posts · brand stories · deck (if built). Flag each: consistent / minor drift / major inconsistency. Name the exact fix for every drift.
props.runningText: make every major fix before Episode 4 — the deck builds on a coherent foundation…

beats.narration_text: Assemble and Audit for Coherence. Liam, in for Bear.
